shortDescription.text: Server-Side Request Forgery allowing internal network probing or cloud metadata access
Missing URL scheme validation allows non-HTTP protocols (file://, gopher://) to bypass SSRF proxy
Double-fetch race condition between HEAD validation and GET download
Error messages expose internal URL and response content to the client

message.text: The GetRemoteFileInfo endpoint accepts a user-controlled URL in the path and issues HEAD/GET requests via ssrf_proxy. SSRF protection relies entirely on external Squid proxy configuration. If the proxy is misconfigured, absent, or bypassed (e.g., via DNS rebinding), an attacker can probe internal services (169.254.169.254 cloud metadata, localhost services, internal APIs). The URL is taken directly from the path parameter with only URL-decoding applied.

The RemoteFileInfoApi (web) endpoint has the same SSRF pattern as the console variant. A user-supplied URL path parameter is decoded and used to make HEAD/GET requests. The web endpoint is accessible to end users (not just console admins), widening the attack surface. Protection depends on external proxy infrastructure.

The console RemoteFileUploadPayload defines url as a bare 'str' field with no URL scheme validation. An attacker could supply file://, gopher://, or other non-HTTP schemes. While httpx may reject some schemes, the lack of application-level validation means the defense is implicit and fragile. The web endpoint uses Pydantic HttpUrl which enforces http/https schemes, but the console endpoint does not. This inconsistency suggests the console endpoint was not hardened intentionally.

The RemoteFileUpload (console) endpoint fetches content from a user-supplied URL. The ssrf_proxy delegates protection to an external Squid proxy that may or may not be deployed. The proxy detects Squid via Server/Via headers (status 401/403), but a non-Squid proxy or direct connection would bypass this detection entirely. The get_remote_file_info() helper in remote.py also makes HEAD requests to arbitrary URLs for metadata resolution in workflow contexts.

The RemoteFileUploadApi (web) endpoint has identical SSRF exposure. It fetches user-supplied URLs and downloads their content. The web endpoint is exposed to end users of deployed Dify apps, not just admin console users, making exploitation more accessible.

The get_remote_file_info() factory helper in remote.py issues HEAD requests to arbitrary URLs using ssrf_proxy for metadata resolution. This function is used in workflow file normalization paths, meaning any workflow that processes remote file references will trigger server-side requests. The URL is not validated for scheme or destination before the request is made.

Both upload endpoints perform a HEAD request to check file metadata (size, type), then a separate GET request to download content. Between these two requests, the remote resource could change — an attacker could serve a small benign file for HEAD, then a large malicious payload for GET, bypassing size and type validation. The console endpoint line 'content = resp.content if resp.request.method == "GET" else ssrf_proxy.get(url).content' explicitly shows the double-fetch pattern.

Error messages in both upload endpoints include the user-supplied URL and the response text from the remote server (resp.text). If an attacker targets an internal service, the error response body may leak internal service information, headers, or error pages back to the attacker. Example: 'raise RemoteFileUploadError(f"Failed to fetch file from {url}: {resp.text}")' exposes the response body of internal services.
